1.1: 12-14. The call to survey Zion, or the Church, as a fortified city, is designed to suggest "how well our God secures His fold." This security is perpetual, and its pledge is His guidance through this life.
1.7:
4. incline--

to hear attentively ( Psa 17:6 ; 31:2 ). In Hebrew and Greek "parable" and "proverb" are translations of the same word. It denotes a comparison, or form of speech, which under one image includes many, and is expressive of a general truth capable of various illustrations. Hence it may be used for the illustration itself. For the former sense, "proverb" (that is, one word for several) is the usual English term, and for the latter, in which comparison is prominent, "parable" (that is, one thing laid by another). The distinction is not always observed, since here, and in Psa 78:2 ; "proverb" would better express the style of the composition (compare also Pr 26:7 , 9 ; Ha 2:6 ; Joh 16:25 , 29 ). Such forms of speech are often very figurative and also obscure (compare Mt 13:12-15 ). Hence the use of the parallel word-- or, "riddle" (compare Eze 17:2 ). is to explain. the accompaniment for a lyric.
1.13: 7-9. yet unable to save themselves or others.

2.1:
14. Like sheep--

(compare Psa 49:12 ) unwittingly, they or, "put," &c. or, better, "shall rule" as a shepherd (compare "feed," Psa 28:9 , Margin). or, "subdue" suddenly, or in their turn. literally, "form" or shape. literally, "is for the consumption," that is, of the grave. literally, "from their home (they go) to it," that is, the grave.
2.7: 16-19. applies this instruction. Be not anxious ( Psa 37:1 , &c.), since death cuts off the prosperous wicked whom you dread.
2.13: 1-4. The description of this majestic appearance of God resembles that of His giving the law (compare Ex 19:16 ; 20:18 ; De 32:1 ).

3.1:
8-15. However scrupulous in external worship, it was offered as if they conferred an obligation in giving God His own, and with a degrading view of Him as needing it [Psa 50:9-13]. Reproving them for such foolish and blasphemous notions, He teaches them to offer, or literally, "sacrifice," thanksgiving, and pay, or perform, their vows--

that is, to bring, with the external symbolical service, the homage of the heart, and faith, penitence, and love. To this is added an invitation to seek, and a promise to afford, all needed help in trouble.
3.7:
8-15. However scrupulous in external worship, it was offered as if they conferred an obligation in giving God His own, and with a degrading view of Him as needing it [Psa 50:9-13]. Reproving them for such foolish and blasphemous notions, He teaches them to offer, or literally, "sacrifice," thanksgiving, and pay, or perform, their vows--

that is, to bring, with the external symbolical service, the homage of the heart, and faith, penitence, and love. To this is added an invitation to seek, and a promise to afford, all needed help in trouble.

4.1:
16-20. the wicked--

that is, the formalists, as now exposed, and who lead vicious lives (compare Ro 2:21 , 23). They are unworthy to use even the words of God's law. Their hypocrisy and vice are exposed by illustrations from sins against the seventh, eighth, and ninth commandments.
4.7:
23. offereth praise--

( Psa 50:14 ), so that the external worship is a true index of the heart. acts in a straight, right manner, opposed to turning aside ( Psa 25:5 ). In such, pure worship and a pure life evince their true piety, and they will enjoy God's presence and favor.
4.13:
blot out--

1-4. A plea for mercy is a confession of guilt. as from a register. literally, "rebellions" ( Psa 19:13 ; 32:1 ).

5.2:
Purge ... hyssop--

7-12. A series of prayers for forgiveness and purifying. The use of this plant in the ritual ( Ex 12:22 ; Nu 19:6 , 18 ) suggests the idea of atonement as prominent here; "purge" refers to vicarious satisfaction ( Nu 19:17-20 ).
5.8:
14. Deliver--

or, "Free me" ( Psa 39:8 ) from the guilt of murder ( 2Sa 12:9 , 10 ; Psa 5:6 ). as in Psa 7:17 ; 31:1 .
5.14: 19. God reconciled, material sacrifices will be acceptable ( Psa 4:5 ; compare Is 1:11-17 ).

6.3:
4. all-devouring--

literally, "swallowing," which utterly destroy (compare Psa 21:9 ; 35:25 ).
6.9:
9. hast done--

that is, what the context supplies, "preserved me" (compare Psa 22:31 ). hope in Thy perfections, manifested for my good ( Psa 5:11 ; 20:1 ). that is, Thy name, and the whole method or result of its manifestation ( Psa 54:6 ; 69:16 ).

7.1: 1-4. with few verbal changes, correspond with Psa 14:1-4 .
7.13:
7. mine eye ... desire--

(compare Psa 59:10 ; 112:8 ), expresses satisfaction in beholding the overthrow of his enemies as those of God, without implying any selfish or unholy feeling (compare Psa 52:6 , 7 ).

8.11:
9. Destroy--

literally, "swallow" ( Psa 21:9 ). or, "confound their speech," and hence their counsels ( Ge 11:7 ). perhaps Jerusalem, the scene of anarchy.
